message.content:
Adversarially verify this finding about a design doc (the doc is at /tmp/claude-1000/-home-jleechan-projects-worktree-runner/8ca589dd-8422-44b1-a543-52bd7b71f14b/scratchpad/gistfile1.txt; the repo is /home/jleechan/projects/worktree_runner; you may use WebSearch/WebFetch and read files). Try to REFUTE it. Finding from the "rust" reviewer, severity major:
TITLE: Stringly-typed Platform (os: String) with runtime string comparisons instead of an enum
DETAIL: src/platform.rs lines 157–183 model the OS as `String` ("macos"/"linux"/"unknown") and backend.rs compares with `plat.os == "macos"` (line 196). The cfg! macro is compile-time constant, so this whole if/else chain collapses to a single known string per build — the runtime representation adds typo risk (no compiler help if backend.rs checks "macOS") and an 'unknown' state that then flows into backend selection (see the unreachable-Docker finding). The design targets exactly two OSes for v1; that is an enum, not a string.
RECOMMENDATION: `enum Os { MacOs, Linux }` and make `detect()` return `Result<Platform>` that errors on unsupported targets. Alternatively use conditional compilation (`#[cfg(target_os = ...)]` modules) so the macOS build doesn't even contain libvirt selection code. Keep `arch` as `std::env::consts::ARCH` but consider an `enum Arch { Aarch64, X86_64 }` since Tart is Apple-Silicon-only per the design (line 14) — the current code would happily pick Tart on an Intel Mac if tart is on PATH.
Is the finding factually correct and does the recommendation make sense? If the factual basis is wrong or the severity is inflated, refute it. Default to refuted=true only if you have concrete contrary evidence or the claim is unsupported after checking.

input.description: List repo src dir and check doc length

input.reasoning: Every factual claim checks out against the doc. (1) Platform struct at doc lines 158–163 models os as String; detect() (lines 165–183) assigns "macos"/"linux"/"unknown" via a cfg! chain, and cfg! is indeed compile-time constant, so the runtime string encodes only build-time information. (2) backend.rs line 196 compares `plat.os == "macos"` exactly as quoted, with no compiler protection against typos. (3) The "unknown" value does flow into select(), falling through to Backend::Docker (line 205), tying into the unreachable-Docker finding. (4) The design's v1 scope (doc lines 13–17) is exactly macOS + Linux, so an enum is the natural model. (5) Doc line 14 restricts Tart to Apple Silicon, and select() never consults plat.arch — the arch field is collected (line 177) but unused — so the code would select Tart on any macOS with tart on PATH. The only weakness is practical: Tart cannot actually run on Intel Macs, so that specific scenario is nearly impossible in the field, but the code-level observation (arch unchecked) remains true. The recommendation (enum Os, Result-returning detect, or cfg-gated modules, plus an Arch enum) is idiomatic Rust and consistent with the design's stated scope. Severity "major" is defensible for a design doc since the stringly-typed Platform is the foundational data model for backend selection and directly creates the undefined "unknown" path. No concrete contrary evidence found.
